The AI infrastructure story is far from over, and photonics is becoming an increasingly important piece of the puzzle. As AI training and inference workloads continue to grow, data movement—not compute itself—is emerging as the next major bottleneck. 
 
AAOI, LITE, and COHR benefit from rising demand for optical modules, lasers, and high-speed interconnects. Investors spent years focusing on GPUs. The next phase may be about moving data faster and more efficiently. If AI spending continues to expand, the photonics ecosystem could become one of the most important growth themes of the next decade.

Jensen Huang’s AI evolution framework (Generative → Agentic → Physical AI) highlights a structural shift: the real bottleneck is gradually moving from compute chips toward materials, optics, and high-speed interconnect infrastructure. 
 
From this perspective, names like AXTI, GLW, and ALMU (materials), as well as LASR and LPTH (laser/photonics components), are essentially leverage plays on the expanding AI data wave and the ongoing buildout of large-scale data centers and electro-optical convergence. 
 
However, the market is entering a more sensitive phase: many of these stocks have already delivered multi-bagger moves, meaning short-term performance is increasingly driven by narrative and sentiment rather than fundamental earnings realization. 
 
The key question: will materials and photonics become the next AI leadership leg, or will capital rotate back to higher-certainty leaders like NVDA and AVGO?

Corning Glass will benefit the most from this obvious need. They are the premier light/glass company of the world and have blocking patents that cover many aspects in optics, photonics, lithography, chip fabrication and even quantum computing. Corning is an EASY 20x to $4T when fiber optics and photonics are incorporated in virtually every part of the datacenter deployments process. Buy Corning now or live with your regret.

Nvidia poured $18.6B into venture-capital-like investments in just one quarter, raising questions about where its cash is really flowing. The company generated $48.6B in free cash flow, but also held $42.3B in nonmarketable equity securities, up sharply from $3.2B a year earlier, suggesting a growing portion of capital is tied up in illiquid private investments.

The $18.58B spent on nonmarketable equity securities is not counted as capex, which makes free cash flow ($48.6B) appear more readily available than it may actually be.

"The Warrants are exercisable at any time on or after the issuance date. The Traditional Warrant will expire on the earliest to occur of (a) the third anniversary of the issuance date, (b) the termination of the definitive agreement governing the long-term partnership, subject to certain exceptions, and (c) the consummation of a fundamental transaction by the Company. The Pre-Funded Warrant will expire on the earlier to occur of (x) the third anniversary of the issuance date and (y) the consummation of a fundamental transaction by the Company. The exercise price and the number of shares of Common Stock issuable upon exercise of the Warrants will be subject to customary structural anti-dilution adjustments. Pursuant to the Pre-Funded Warrant, NVIDIA will be entitled to participate on an as-exercised basis in distributions to holders of the Common Stock," the filing stated.

$GLW — tightening right on the edge of the base now 👀📦 
This is classic compression behavior: price coils up, volatility shrinks, and the structure starts to “pin” near the highs of the range. That’s usually where something bigger is getting priced in. 
Investor Day on Wednesday is the real catalyst here. Market doesn’t stay this tight for long when an event is sitting right on top of it. 
Two clean paths: 
• Break and hold above the base → potential expansion phase, momentum can stack quickly 
• Fail and rotate back into the range → another reset, back to chop and mean reversion 
What stands out is the positioning: this isn’t drifting lower into the event, it’s tightening at the right edge. That matters. 
Let’s see if Wednesday turns compression into expansion or just another range cycle.

Corning shares fell sharply after comments from Hock E. Tan, CEO of Broadcom, who suggested the transition from copper to fiber within AI data-center racks may not occur in the near term. The remarks weighed on investor expectations for optical networking, a key growth driver tied to the AI boom. 
Corning previously partnered with Broadcom on co-packaged optics , a technology designed to connect AI chips using optical fiber instead of copper to improve power efficiency and processing capacity. However, Tan said direct-attached copper still offers lower latency, lower power consumption, and lower cost, making it the preferred option for rack-level connections for now. 
Corning stock dropped about 8%, marking its biggest single-day decline since April, despite having surged around 65% earlier this year on optimism around AI infrastructure demand.

Hock Tan’s exact comment was specifically about scale-up networking — within a rack or cluster domain — not scale-out (rack-to-rack, building-to-building). The Barron’s article framing conflates the two, which is a meaningful analytical error imo that likely amplified the selloff beyond what the comment warranted. Corning’s and Ciena’s primary near-term optical revenue opportunity is in scale-out — connecting racks and data centers — where fiber has already won and copper is not competitive at those distances. Tan may have deliberately used precise language knowing the market would misread it, buying Broadcom time on its own copper-heavy networking roadmap.
